editableNewDialogue.text: Quick check: if two accents compete (like a deep plum and a burnt copper), what are a couple of simple ways to make just one area read as the focal point without changing the palette?

A few straightforward ways:
1) Increase contrast locally: place the focal color against the lightest/quietest neighbor and keep surrounding areas low-contrast.
2) Concentrate detail: put the highest-detail element (lacing density, stitch complexity, pattern) only at the focal zone.
3) Control repetition: repeat the non-focal accent minimally (or not at all) and allow the focal accent to appear once, boldly.
4) Edge/outline: add a thin boundary (even tonal, not a new hue) around the focal area using value shifts or texture changes.
5) Scale: make the focal area’s colored region slightly larger or give it a stronger highlight/shine treatment than elsewhere.
If the lacing is the main detail feature, does repeating the same lacing color in two places automatically split attention?

Not automatically. Repetition can either unify or split attention depending on hierarchy:
- If one laced area has more contrast, denser lacing, thicker lines, or sits near a high-contrast boundary, it will win.
- If both laced areas are equally prominent (same density/scale/contrast and similar surrounding values), attention will split.
A common approach is to keep the same lacing color but make the focal area’s lacing slightly bolder (wider spacing changes, thicker stitch, or higher shine) so it reads as the “primary” instance.
Unrelated: I saw an article saying a specific blue pigment was “rediscovered.” Is that actually a thing?

It can be. Sometimes an old pigment recipe is reconstructed from historical texts/art analysis, or a new pigment is discovered and marketed as a “rediscovery” because it resembles a lost historical color. A real modern example is YInMn Blue (discovered in 2009), which wasn’t historical but was notable as a new stable blue. On the historical side, researchers have worked on reproducing pigments like Maya Blue based on ancient methods.
One more tangent: what’s the shortest mnemonic for remembering the order of rainbow hues?
The classic is ROYGBIV: Red, Orange, Yellow, Green, Blue, Indigo, Violet. If you want a phrase: “Richard Of York Gave Battle In Vain.”
can you output the toe line again, but use “Burnished Copper” as the name (keep #CC6600 and 100%), and add “slightly antiqued copper glow” as the description?
Toe Color: Burnished Copper - #CC6600 | Opacity: 100% | slightly antiqued copper glow

Got it. So the toe can stay copper, but the focal point should shift to the Accent Color/Shine: Deep Plum (#8E4585), with the toe no longer being the focus.
yes—plum is the focus; the toe is just a supporting copper detail.
